title: Urothelium (Transitional Epithelium)
brick_title: Histology of the Ureters, Bladder, and Urethra
buzzwords: Stratified epithelium impermeable to salts and water
Adapts to massive distention without rupturing
Layers flatten and unfold when stretched
Cell-layer count varies along the urinary tract
Two layers in minor calyces, six-plus in bladder
Loose lamina propria facilitates folding
Surface cells have a characteristic dome shape
Urothelium / transitional epithelium

title: Ureter Histology
brick_title: Histology of the Ureters, Bladder, and Urethra
buzzwords: Paired tubes from renal pelvis to bladder
Inner lining four to five cells thick
Retroperitoneal, so wrapped in adventitia not serosa
Three smooth-muscle layers in the wall
Inner longitudinal, middle circular, outer longitudinal
Outer layer carries vessels, nerves, and adipose
Distinguished by an extra longitudinal muscle layer
Three-layer muscularis of the ureter

title: Urinary Bladder Histology (Detrusor)
brick_title: Histology of the Ureters, Bladder, and Urethra
buzzwords: Hollow organ that stores urine until micturition
Inner lining six or more cell layers thick
Thickest smooth-muscle wall of the urinary tract
Muscle contraction expels stored urine
Superior surface covered by serosa, rest by adventitia
Epithelium thins to three layers when filled
Voluntary contraction drives urination
Detrusor muscle of the bladder

title: Urethra: Male vs Female Epithelial Transitions
brick_title: Histology of the Ureters, Bladder, and Urethra
buzzwords: Conduit from bladder to the outside world
Lining shifts along its length toward skin-like epithelium
Starts transitional, ends stratified squamous
Female version is short and undivided
Male version is ~20 cm with four named segments
Pseudostratified columnar lines the middle of the male tube
Preprostatic, prostatic, membranous, penile segments
Transitional to pseudostratified columnar to stratified squamous
